webmd.author: WebMD
webmd.title: Figwort: Overview, Uses, Side Effects, Precautions, Interactions

medherbals.author: Medicinal Herbals
medherbals.title: Figwort (Scrophularia nodosa): The Natural Skin Healer

actions.action: Traditional 'alterative' for chronic skin conditions - eczema, psoriasis, rashes (as a poultice, salve or tea)

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (aucubin, harpagoside, harpagide, catalpol)
constituents.note: Anti-inflammatory constituents; harpagoside also has cardiac (inotropic) activity.

contraindications.note: Avoid if you have a heart condition or take cardiac medicines: figwort's harpagoside has a stimulating (inotropic) effect on the heart, so it is traditionally avoided with rapid heartbeat or heart disease.
Avoid during pregnancy. There is insufficient reliable evidence on the safety of taking figwort by mouth, so internal use should be cautious and short-term.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from general literature (WebMD and herbal monographs); Scrophularia nodosa is not covered by clinical trials, so efficacy actions are needs-review. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (eczema, psoriasis, skin-irritation); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

ema.author: European Medicines Agency (HMPC)
ema.title: European Union herbal monograph on Gentiana lutea L., radix (Gentianae radix)

olennikov2015.author: Olennikov, D.N., Kashchenko, N.I., Chirikova, N.K. and Tankhaeva, L.M.
olennikov2015.title: Iridoids and Flavonoids of Four Siberian Gentians: Chemical Profile and Gastric Stimulatory Effect

actions.action: Aromatic bitter for temporary loss of appetite
Digestive aid for mild dyspeptic complaints - indigestion, bloating and fullness (stimulates gastric acid, enzyme and bile secretion)
Eases nausea linked to sluggish digestion (traditional)

constituents.name: Secoiridoid bitter glycosides (gentiopicroside, amarogentin, swertiamarin)
constituents.note: Amarogentin is one of the bitterest natural compounds known; these stimulate digestive secretions.

contraindications.note: Because bitters increase stomach-acid secretion, gentian is contraindicated in peptic (stomach or duodenal) ulcer disease and hyperacidity.
Traditional use only; appetite loss or dyspepsia that persists needs medical assessment, and use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from regulatory and scientific literature (EMA HMPC monograph for Gentianae radix; Molecules study of gentian iridoids and gastric stimulation). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (indigestion, bloating, appetite-loss, nausea); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

mandal2020.author: Mandal, S.K., Maji, A.K., Mishra, S.K., Ishfaq, P.M., Devkota, H.P., Silva, A.S. and Das, N.
mandal2020.title: Goldenseal (Hydrastis canadensis L.) and its active constituents: A critical review of their efficacy and toxicological issues

singh2021.author: Singh, S., Pathak, N., Fatima, E. and Negi, A.S.
singh2021.title: Plant isoquinoline alkaloids: Advances in the chemistry and biology of berberine

actions.action: Antimicrobial (berberine) - supports skin, mucosal and gastrointestinal infections and sore throat
Anti-inflammatory and astringent for inflamed mucous membranes (sore-throat gargle, mouth)
Digestive / gastrointestinal support (traditional for dyspepsia and ulcers)
Topical for skin infections and irritation

constituents.name: Isoquinoline alkaloids - berberine (principal), hydrastine and canadine
constituents.note: Berberine is the main antimicrobial, hypoglycaemic and hypolipidaemic constituent.

contraindications.note: Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding: berberine crosses the placenta and into milk and can cause or worsen newborn jaundice (risk of kernicterus); do not give to infants.

Berberine strongly inhibits drug-metabolising enzymes (especially CYP3A4 and CYP2D6), so it can raise the blood levels of many medicines - a significant herb-drug-interaction risk. High doses have shown possible liver, nerve and photo-toxicity.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Pharmacological Research critical review; European Journal of Medicinal Chemistry berberine review). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (infection, sore-throat, indigestion, skin-irritation); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited. NOTE pregnancy/infant contraindication and CYP drug interactions.

bandopadhyay2023.title: Therapeutic properties and pharmacological activities of asiaticoside and madecassoside: A review

belcaro2011.author: Belcaro, G., Maquart, F.-X., Scoccianti, M., Dugall, M., Hosoi, M., Cesarone, M.R., Luzzi, R., Cornelli, U., Ledda, A. and Feragalli, B.
belcaro2011.title: TECA (Titrated Extract of Centella Asiatica): new microcirculatory, biomolecular, and vascular application in preventive and clinical medicine. A status paper

actions.action: Wound healing and scar/collagen support (stimulates collagen synthesis and tissue repair)
Improves venous tone and microcirculation - supports chronic venous insufficiency / varicose veins and reduces leg swelling
Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant; supports inflammatory skin conditions (eczema, psoriasis, ulcers)

constituents.name: Pentacyclic triterpenoid glycosides (asiaticoside, madecassoside) and their aglycones (asiatic and madecassic acid)
constituents.note: The marker compounds and principal actives for wound healing, vascular and neuroprotective effects.

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated topically and orally; it can occasionally cause allergic contact dermatitis and, rarely, reversible elevation of liver enzymes with prolonged high oral doses.
May add to the effect of sedatives; use caution with other liver-affecting medicines. Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, so avoid medicinal doses.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (J Cell Mol Med review of asiaticoside/madecassoside; Panminerva Medica status paper on titrated Centella extract). teschke2012.title: Greater Celandine hepatotoxicity: a clinical review

gilca2010.author: Gilca, M., Gaman, L., Panait, E., Stoian, I. and Atanasiu, V.
gilca2010.title: Chelidonium majus - an integrative review: traditional knowledge versus modern findings

actions.action: Antispasmodic / choleretic bitter for upper-abdominal and biliary dyspepsia (indigestion, bloating, cramping) - oral use now restricted for safety
Fresh orange latex traditionally dabbed on warts

constituents.name: Isoquinoline alkaloids (chelidonine, coptisine, berberine, sanguinarine, chelerythrine)
constituents.note: The biologically active and toxicologically important constituents.

constituents.name: Orange latex (alkaloid-rich sap)
constituents.note: The caustic sap used traditionally on warts.

contraindications.note: SERIOUS: oral greater celandine can cause acute idiosyncratic liver injury (hepatocellular hepatotoxicity with jaundice). Multiple reported cases led regulators to restrict or suspend oral products. Do not take internally if you have any liver disease, and stop immediately and seek care at any sign of liver problems (jaundice, dark urine, upper-abdominal pain).
Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding. The fresh orange latex is irritant and caustic - keep it away from the eyes and broken skin.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Annals of Hepatology clinical review of hepatotoxicity; Forschende Komplementarmedizin integrative review). Record status set to needs-review because oral use carries a documented hepatotoxicity risk that regulators have restricted. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (indigestion, bloating, warts); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.
